 {"id":"bd-1mh","title":"Implement HitGrid for mouse hit testing","description":"PHASE 5 - HIT TESTING INFRASTRUCTURE

## Background
Mouse interaction requires knowing what widget is at a given coordinate. HitGrid provides a parallel grid that maps screen positions to interactive elements.

## Technical Requirements

### HitGrid Structure
pub struct HitGrid {
    width: u16,
    height: u16,
    cells: Vec<HitCell>,
}

pub struct HitCell {
    /// Widget ID at this position
    widget_id: Option<WidgetId>,
    /// Interactive region type
    region: HitRegion,
    /// User data for callbacks
    data: HitData,
}

pub enum HitRegion {
    None,
    Content,        // Main widget content
    Border,         // Widget border
    Scrollbar,      // Scrollbar area
    Handle,         // Resize handle
    Button,         // Clickable button
    Link,           // Hyperlink
    Custom(u8),     // User-defined
}

pub type WidgetId = u32;
pub type HitData = u64;  // Opaque user data

### Registration API
impl HitGrid {
    /// Clear all hit regions
    pub fn clear(&mut self);
    
    /// Register a hit region
    pub fn register(&mut self, area: Rect, widget_id: WidgetId, region: HitRegion, data: HitData);
    
    /// Query hit at position
    pub fn hit_test(&self, x: u16, y: u16) -> Option<(WidgetId, HitRegion, HitData)>;
    
    /// Query all hits in area
    pub fn hits_in(&self, area: Rect) -> Vec<(WidgetId, HitRegion, HitData)>;
}

### Frame Integration
pub struct Frame {
    buffer: Buffer,
    hit_grid: Option<HitGrid>,  // Optional for performance
}

impl Frame {
    pub fn enable_hit_testing(&mut self);
    
    pub fn register_hit(&mut self, area: Rect, widget_id: WidgetId, region: HitRegion, data: HitData) {
        if let Some(ref mut grid) = self.hit_grid {
            grid.register(area, widget_id, region, data);
        }
    }
}

### Widget Integration Pattern
impl Widget for Button {
    fn render(self, area: Rect, buf: &mut Buffer) {
        // Render button...
        
        // Register hit area
        buf.frame().register_hit(
            area,
            self.widget_id,
            HitRegion::Button,
            self.on_click_data,
        );
    }
}

### Event Dispatch
impl Program {
    fn handle_mouse(&mut self, event: MouseEvent) {
        if let Some((widget_id, region, data)) = self.frame.hit_test(event.x, event.y) {
            // Dispatch to appropriate handler
        }
    }
}

## Tests Required
- [ ] Registration works
- [ ] Hit test returns correct widget
- [ ] Overlapping regions: last wins
- [ ] Clear removes all hits
- [ ] hits_in returns all in area
- [ ] Performance: 80x24 grid operations < 1µs

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] HitGrid implementation
- [ ] Frame integration
- [ ] Widget registration pattern
- [ ] Mouse event dispatch
- [ ] Optional (feature gated) for performance

 {"id":"bd-31q3","title":"Text Effects: Particle Dissolve / Materialize","description":"## Background
Particle dissolve creates dramatic transitions where text breaks into fragments that drift away, or materializes from scattered particles. Builds on existing SparkleField infrastructure.

## Implementation

Add DissolveMode enum:
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, Default)]
pub enum DissolveMode {
    #[default]
    Dissolve,    // Text breaks apart, drifts away
    Materialize, // Particles gather into text
    Explode,     // Fast outward burst from center
    Implode,     // Fast inward gather to center
}
```

Add TextEffect variant:
```rust
TextEffect::ParticleDissolve {
    progress: f64,        // 0.0 = solid text, 1.0 = fully particles
    mode: DissolveMode,   
    speed: f64,           // Particle drift speed
    gravity: f64,         // 0.0 = float, 1.0 = fall downward
    seed: u64,            // Deterministic particle paths
}
```

### Particle Behavior:
```rust
struct Particle {
    origin: (f64, f64),     // Original text position
    offset: (f64, f64),     // Current offset from origin
    char: char,             // Original character
    color: PackedRgba,      // Inherited from text color
}

// Particles inherit color from the text effect chain
// e.g., Rainbow + Dissolve = rainbow-colored particles
```

### Rendering by Mode:

| Mode | progress=0 | progress=0.5 | progress=1 |
|------|------------|--------------|------------|
| Dissolve | Solid text | Half scattered | All particles |
| Materialize | All particles | Half gathered | Solid text |
| Explode | Solid text | Burst outward | Distant particles |
| Implode | Distant particles | Gathering | Solid text |

### Particle Characters:
Default particle chars: `['·', '∙', '•', '*']` - selected based on distance from origin (closer = larger).

### Color Inheritance:
Particles inherit their color from whatever color the text would have at that position. This means gradients, color cycling, etc. all work with dissolve.

## Files
crates/ftui-extras/src/text_effects.rs

## Unit Tests (REQUIRED)
- test_dissolve_progress_0_solid: All original chars visible
- test_dissolve_progress_1_scattered: No chars at original positions
- test_materialize_reverses: progress 0->1 gathers particles
- test_explode_from_center: Particles move away from text center
- test_implode_to_center: Particles converge on center
- test_gravity_affects_y: Particles drift downward with gravity
- test_particle_inherits_color: Gradient text = gradient particles
- test_seed_deterministic: Same seed = same particle paths

## Visual Tests
- Snapshot 'DISSOLVE' at progress 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0
- Snapshot Explode burst pattern
- Snapshot gradient text dissolving (particles should be colorful)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Deliver the behavior implied by the title: Text Effects: Particle Dissolve / Materialize
- Alien Artifact: define invariants + failure modes; if heuristics exist, specify an explainable decision rule (evidence ledger / Bayes factors where applicable)
- Extreme Optimization: baseline -> profile -> opportunity matrix; for each perf change include an isomorphism proof + golden output checksums
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures; add property tests for core invariants where applicable
- E2E: scripts with verbose JSONL logging (env, capabilities, timings, seed, checksums) + deterministic mode

 {"id":"bd-3227","title":"Terminal Capability Auto-Upgrade with Safe Probing","description":"# Terminal Capability Auto-Upgrade with Safe Probing

## Background & Motivation

The plan (Section 3.4) describes TerminalCapabilities detection via environment variables (TERM, COLORTERM, etc). This is static and conservative - it often underestimates modern terminals.

**The problem:**
- iTerm2 supports truecolor, synchronized output, and OSC 8 - but TERM=xterm-256color
- WezTerm and Kitty support advanced features not detectable from env
- Users get degraded experiences unnecessarily

**The solution:**
- Start with conservative env-based detection (existing approach)
- Probe for capabilities with bounded timeouts
- Upgrade capabilities as terminal proves support
- Never degrade unexpectedly

**Relationship to existing beads:**
- Extends bd-3ky.11 (runtime capability probing)
- Enhances bd-10i.4.4 (TerminalCapabilities detection)

## Technical Design

### Probeable Capabilities

```rust
/// Capabilities that can be probed at runtime
#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
pub enum ProbeableCapability {
    /// TrueColor (24-bit) support
    TrueColor,
    /// Synchronized output (mode 2026)
    SynchronizedOutput,
    /// OSC 8 hyperlinks
    Hyperlinks,
    /// Kitty keyboard protocol
    KittyKeyboard,
    /// Sixel graphics
    Sixel,
    /// iTerm2 inline images
    ItermImages,
}

/// Result of a capability probe
#[derive(Clone, Copy)]
pub enum ProbeResult {
    /// Terminal confirmed support
    Confirmed,
    /// Terminal explicitly denied
    Denied,
    /// No response within timeout
    Timeout,
    /// Probe not yet sent
    Pending,
}
```

### Capability Prober

```rust
pub struct CapabilityProber {
    /// Confirmed capabilities
    confirmed: HashSet<ProbeableCapability>,
    /// Denied capabilities
    denied: HashSet<ProbeableCapability>,
    /// Pending probes with send time
    pending: HashMap<ProbeId, (Instant, ProbeableCapability)>,
    /// Timeout for probe responses
    timeout: Duration,
    /// Probe counter for unique IDs
    probe_counter: u32,
}

impl CapabilityProber {
    pub fn new(timeout: Duration) -> Self {
        Self {
            confirmed: HashSet::new(),
            denied: HashSet::new(),
            pending: HashMap::new(),
            timeout,
            probe_counter: 0,
        }
    }
    
    /// Send a probe and return token for tracking
    pub fn probe(&mut self, cap: ProbeableCapability, writer: &mut impl Write) -> io::Result<ProbeId> {
        let id = ProbeId(self.probe_counter);
        self.probe_counter += 1;
        
        // Send appropriate probe sequence
        let probe_bytes = match cap {
            ProbeableCapability::TrueColor => {
                // Query using DA2 or similar
                b"\\x1b[>c"
            }
            ProbeableCapability::SynchronizedOutput => {
                // Query mode 2026
                b"\\x1b[?2026$p"
            }
            ProbeableCapability::Hyperlinks => {
                // OSC 8 query (custom, terminal-specific)
                b"\\x1b]8;id=probe;\\x07"
            }
            // ... other probes
        };
        
        writer.write_all(probe_bytes)?;
        writer.flush()?;
        
        self.pending.insert(id, (Instant::now(), cap));
        Ok(id)
    }
    
    /// Process response bytes, updating capability status
    pub fn process_response(&mut self, response: &[u8]) {
        // Parse response and match to pending probes
        // This is capability-specific
        if let Some((id, cap)) = self.parse_probe_response(response) {
            if let Some((_, _)) = self.pending.remove(&id) {
                self.confirmed.insert(cap);
            }
        }
    }
    
    /// Check for timed-out probes
    pub fn check_timeouts(&mut self) {
        let now = Instant::now();
        let timed_out: Vec<_> = self.pending
            .iter()
            .filter(|(_, (sent, _))| now.duration_since(*sent) > self.timeout)
            .map(|(id, (_, cap))| (*id, *cap))
            .collect();
            
        for (id, _cap) in timed_out {
            self.pending.remove(&id);
            // Timeout = assume not supported (conservative)
        }
    }
    
    /// Check if capability is confirmed
    pub fn is_confirmed(&self, cap: ProbeableCapability) -> bool {
        self.confirmed.contains(&cap)
    }
    
    /// Get upgrade recommendation
    pub fn upgrades(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = ProbeableCapability> + '_ {
        self.confirmed.iter().copied()
    }
}
```

### Integration with TerminalCapabilities

```rust
impl TerminalCapabilities {
    /// Start capability probing (call once after entering raw mode)
    pub fn start_probing(&mut self, writer: &mut impl Write) -> io::Result<()> {
        if \!self.probing_enabled {
            return Ok(());
        }
        
        // Probe capabilities we dont already have from env
        for cap in ProbeableCapability::all() {
            if \!self.has(cap.into()) {
                self.prober.probe(cap, writer)?;
            }
        }
        Ok(())
    }
    
    /// Process incoming data for probe responses
    pub fn process_input(&mut self, data: &[u8]) {
        self.prober.process_response(data);
        self.prober.check_timeouts();
        
        // Apply upgrades
        for cap in self.prober.upgrades() {
            self.upgrade(cap.into());
            tracing::info\!(
                capability = ?cap,
                "Terminal capability confirmed via probe"
            );
        }
    }
    
    /// Upgrade a capability (one-way: never downgrade)
    fn upgrade(&mut self, cap: Capability) {
        self.capabilities.insert(cap);
    }
}
```

### Probe Response Parsing

```rust
impl CapabilityProber {
    fn parse_probe_response(&self, data: &[u8]) -> Option<(ProbeId, ProbeableCapability)> {
        // DA2 response: ESC [ > Pp ; Pv ; Pc c
        if data.starts_with(b"\\x1b[>") && data.ends_with(b"c") {
            // Parse terminal type and version
            // iTerm2, WezTerm, Kitty have specific signatures
            return self.parse_da2_response(data);
        }
        
        // Mode query response: ESC [ ? 2026 ; Ps $ y
        if data.contains(&b) && data.ends_with(b"y") {
            return self.parse_mode_response(data);
        }
        
        None
    }
    
    fn parse_da2_response(&self, data: &[u8]) -> Option<(ProbeId, ProbeableCapability)> {
        // Known terminal signatures
        // iTerm2: 0;95 or similar
        // Kitty: 1;4000;... 
        // WezTerm: specific pattern
        
        // If we recognize the terminal, confirm its known capabilities
        // ...
    }
}
```

### Safety Guarantees

1. **Bounded timeout**: Probes never hang indefinitely
2. **One-way upgrade**: Never downgrade capabilities
3. **Conservative default**: Timeout = assume unsupported
4. **Non-blocking**: Probes are async, dont block input
5. **Graceful degradation**: If probing fails, use env detection

Add a DOT (GraphViz) parser that produces the same MermaidDiagramIr, enabling all existing layout and rendering code to work with DOT input. DOT is the other dominant text-based graph format used by many tools (doxygen, profilers, dependency analyzers, terraform).

## Why
- Broadens utility significantly: any tool that outputs DOT can now be visualized in the terminal.
- The IR is already format-agnostic — a DOT parser just needs to produce IrNodes, IrEdges, IrClusters.
- DOT is simpler to parse than Mermaid (well-defined grammar, no ambiguity).
- Enables workflows like: cargo depgraph | ftui-diagram (visualize Rust crate dependencies).

## DOT Subset to Support (v1)
- graph/digraph declarations
- Node declarations with labels, shapes, colors
- Edge declarations (-> for digraph, -- for graph)
- subgraph/cluster declarations
- Basic attributes: label, shape, color, style, fillcolor
- DOT shape → NodeShape mapping: box→Rect, diamond→Diamond, circle→Circle, ellipse→Rounded, etc.

## DOT Features to Skip (v1)
- HTML labels, record shapes, rank constraints, edge ports, DOT layout hints (pos/neato)
- These can be added later; the basic pipeline is the priority.

## Implementation
- New module: crates/ftui-extras/src/dot_parser.rs (or add to mermaid.rs behind cfg)
- Entry point: parse_dot(input: &str) → Result<MermaidDiagramIr, ...>
- Reuse IrNode, IrEdge, IrCluster, IrLabel types from mermaid.rs
- Auto-detect format: if input starts with "graph" or "digraph", use DOT parser; if "flowchart"/"graph TD"/etc., use Mermaid parser.

## Files
- crates/ftui-extras/src/dot_parser.rs (NEW file — genuinely new parser, not a variation)
- crates/ftui-extras/src/lib.rs (feature-gate and export)
- crates/ftui-extras/src/mermaid_render.rs (auto-detect input format)

## Tests
- Unit: parse simple digraph → correct IR nodes/edges
- Unit: parse subgraph → correct clusters
- Unit: DOT shape attributes → correct NodeShape
- Snapshot: cargo dependency graph (10-20 nodes) rendered from DOT
- E2E: round-trip DOT→IR→render produces deterministic output","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-05T15:28:44.556251604Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T18:02:32.537615405Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["graphviz","mermaid","parser"]}

 {"id":"bd-32lt","title":"Semantic Events: Widget Migration Guide and Examples","description":"## Task

Create documentation and examples showing how to use semantic events in widgets.

## Documentation

Create `docs/semantic-events.md`:

1. Overview of semantic vs raw events
2. When to use which
3. Configuration options
4. Migration guide for existing widgets

## Example: List with Double-Click

```rust
enum Msg {
    // Raw events
    Key(KeyEvent),
    Mouse(MouseEvent),
    
    // Semantic events
    ItemClicked(usize),
    ItemDoubleClicked(usize),
    ItemDragged { from: usize, to: usize },
}

impl Message for Msg {
    fn from_semantic(event: SemanticEvent) -> Option<Self> {
        match event {
            SemanticEvent::DoubleClick { pos, .. } => {
                // Convert position to item index
                Some(Msg::ItemDoubleClicked(pos.y as usize))
            }
            SemanticEvent::DragEnd { start, end } => {
                Some(Msg::ItemDragged { 
                    from: start.y as usize, 
                    to: end.y as usize 
                })
            }
            _ => None
        }
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Comprehensive documentation
- [ ] Migration guide
- [ ] 3+ working examples
- [ ] Updated in demo showcase

## Alien Artifact Upgrades
- Model the core decision(s) with explicit state, priors/assumptions, and invariants; write down failure modes as first-class artifacts.
- Explainability: add an evidence ledger for any non-trivial decision (why this branch, why this fallback). Prefer Bayes factors / log-likelihood contributions over ad-hoc scoring.
- Guarantees: where prediction/thresholding exists, prefer distribution-free calibration (conformal prediction) or anytime-valid testing (e-process) so we can stop early without invalid inference.
- Graceful degradation: define capability tiers + deterministic fallbacks; never fail closed without a traceable reason.


## Extreme Optimization
- Baseline first: capture p50/p95/p99 for relevant command(s) with `hyperfine` (store raw output).
- Profile next: CPU flamegraph + allocation profile; identify top-5 hotspots.
- Opportunity matrix: implement only Score ≥ 2.0 (Impact×Confidence/Effort).
- Isomorphism proof per change: ordering, tie-breaking, RNG seeds, and golden checksums must match.
- Regression gates: add microbench + budgets; failures must print actionable deltas and include log artifacts.


## Testing Plan
- Unit tests: happy path + edge cases + regression fixtures.
- Property tests: invariants (idempotence, monotonicity, determinism) where applicable.
- Fuzz tests: parsers/state machines and any input-facing logic.
- Determinism: fixed seeds/time-freeze; snapshot fixtures must be stable.


## E2E Plan
- Provide/extend an E2E script that exercises the feature end-to-end in PTY (where applicable).
- Logging: verbose JSONL with env/capabilities + per-step timings + seed + checksums; include a summary at end.
- On failure: print reproduction command and point to artifacts directory.

 {"id":"bd-3o1l","title":"Text Effects: Mirror / Reflection","description":"## Background
Mirror/reflection creates a classic 'glossy' text effect with a vertically flipped copy below. Works especially well for titles using AsciiArtText, terminal-appropriate since it uses vertical space.

## Implementation

Add Reflection struct (separate from TextEffect for flexibility):
```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
pub struct Reflection {
    pub gap: u16,            // Rows between text and reflection (0-3 typical)
    pub opacity_start: f64,  // Top of reflection (0.3-0.5 typical)
    pub opacity_end: f64,    // Bottom of reflection (0.0 typical)
    pub height_ratio: f64,   // 0.0-1.0, how much of text height to reflect
    pub wave: f64,           // 0.0=perfect mirror, 1.0=wavy water effect
}

impl Default for Reflection {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            gap: 1,
            opacity_start: 0.4,
            opacity_end: 0.0,
            height_ratio: 0.5,  // Reflect bottom half of text
            wave: 0.0,
        }
    }
}
```

### Usage with StyledText:
```rust
// Only works with multi-line text (AsciiArtText or actual multi-line)
let art = AsciiArtText::new("TITLE", AsciiArtStyle::Block);
StyledText::from_ascii_art(art)
    .reflection(Reflection::default())
    .effect(TextEffect::HorizontalGradient { ... })
```

### Single-Line Behavior:
For single-line text, reflection is a no-op (nothing to flip vertically). Use AsciiArtText for reflections on titles.

### Rendering:
```rust
fn render_with_reflection(&self, area: Rect, frame: &mut Frame) {
    // 1. Render main text
    self.render_text(main_area, frame);
    
    // 2. Calculate reflection area
    let reflect_height = (self.text_height as f64 * self.reflection.height_ratio) as u16;
    let reflect_y = main_area.bottom() + self.reflection.gap;
    
    // 3. Render flipped text with gradient opacity
    for row in 0..reflect_height {
        let source_row = self.text_height - 1 - row;  // Flip vertically
        let opacity = lerp(self.reflection.opacity_start, 
                          self.reflection.opacity_end,
                          row as f64 / reflect_height as f64);
        
        let wave_offset = if self.reflection.wave > 0.0 {
            (row as f64 * 0.5 + self.time * 2.0).sin() * self.reflection.wave
        } else { 0.0 };
        
        self.render_row_with_opacity(source_row, reflect_y + row, opacity, wave_offset, frame);
    }
}
```

 {"id":"bd-ukp1f","title":"Sub-Cell Canvas Diagram Rendering","description":"## Goal
Render Mermaid diagrams using FrankenTUI's existing Canvas system (Braille 2×4, Block 2×2, HalfBlock 1×2) instead of cell-by-cell line-drawing characters. This gives 2-8x spatial resolution, enabling smooth diagonal edges, recognizable geometric shapes (real circles/diamonds/hexagons), proper arrowhead triangles, and filled regions.

## Why This Is Transformative
- The Canvas system ALREADY EXISTS (canvas.rs) with Painter, line/circle/rect/fill primitives, and 3 rendering modes.
- Currently the mermaid renderer writes line-drawing chars one cell at a time — max 1:1 pixel resolution.
- Sub-cell rendering turns diagrams from "ASCII art" to "terminal graphics" — a qualitative visual leap.
- Naturally solves shape rendering (circles become actual circles in Braille mode).
- Solves curved edges for free (Painter draws lines at arbitrary angles).
- Deterministic (no floating-point variance), fits FrankenTUI's correctness philosophy.

## Implementation Strategy
1. Add CanvasMermaidRenderer alongside existing cell-based MermaidRenderer in mermaid_render.rs.
2. Reuse Viewport transform (layout coords → canvas coords).
3. Use Painter for: filled rects (nodes), lines (edges), circles (round nodes), polygons (diamonds/hexagons), triangles (arrowheads).
4. Overlay text labels on the Buffer cell layer (Canvas + text compositing already works in canvas.rs).
5. Feature-gated behind "diagram" feature, rendering mode selectable via config.
6. Auto-detect best mode from TerminalCapabilities (Braille if unicode supported, Block/HalfBlock as fallbacks).

## Key Design Decisions
- Canvas rendering is an ALTERNATIVE to cell rendering, not a replacement. Both modes must work.
- Layout coordinates remain the same — only the rendering backend changes.
- Text labels always use cell-based rendering (you can't write text in Braille dots).
- Node borders + fills use Canvas; labels use Buffer cell writes — they composite naturally.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Diagrams render in Braille/Block/HalfBlock modes with visually recognizable shapes.
- Edges are smooth lines (not just orthogonal L-bends).
- Arrowheads are triangular, not just > characters.
- Node shapes (circle, diamond, hexagon, etc.) are geometrically correct.
- Text labels remain crisp and readable.
- Rendering mode auto-detected from terminal capabilities.
- All existing tests continue passing (cell mode unchanged).
- New snapshot tests for Canvas mode at 80×24 and 120×40.
- Performance: Canvas rendering within 2× of cell rendering time.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-05T15:25:54.880660705Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-05T15:25:54.880660705Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["canvas","mermaid","rendering"]}
 {"id":"bd-ukp1f.1","title":"Canvas: rendering mode auto-detection + config","description":"## What
Auto-detect the best Canvas rendering mode from terminal capabilities, and provide config override.

## Mode Selection Logic
1. If terminal supports unicode + Braille characters → Braille mode (best: 2×4 resolution per cell)
2. If terminal supports unicode but not Braille → Block mode (2×2 resolution per cell)
3. If terminal supports half-block characters → HalfBlock mode (1×2 resolution per cell)
4. If ASCII only → fall back to cell-based rendering (existing MermaidRenderer)

## Config Override
- Add `render_mode` field to MermaidConfig: Auto | CellOnly | Braille | Block | HalfBlock
- Default: Auto (use terminal detection)

 {"id":"bd-ukp1f.2","title":"Canvas: node shape rendering (all 8 shapes via Painter)","description":"## What
Implement Canvas-based rendering for all 8 NodeShape variants using Painter primitives from canvas.rs.

## Shape Mapping
- Rect → Painter::rect (filled rectangle with border)
- Rounded → Painter::rect with corner radius (approximate with arcs or short diagonals)
- Stadium → Painter::rect with semicircle ends (half-circles at left/right)
- Subroutine → Double-bordered rect (two nested rectangles)
- Diamond → Painter polygon: 4 vertices at (cx, top), (right, cy), (cx, bottom), (left, cy)
- Hexagon → Painter polygon: 6 vertices (flat-top or pointy-top orientation)
- Circle → Painter::circle centered on node, radius = min(width, height) / 2
- Asymmetric → Painter polygon: flag shape (rect with pointed right side)

## Implementation Details
- Each shape gets a dedicated fn: canvas_draw_rect, canvas_draw_diamond, canvas_draw_circle, etc.
- Fill interior with node's theme background color via Painter::fill
- Draw border in node's theme border color via Painter::line or Painter::polygon
- Scale from layout units to canvas pixel coordinates via Viewport transform
- Padding between border and label text must account for shape geometry (diamond has less usable interior than rect)

-{"id":"bd-ukp1f.3","title":"Canvas: text label compositing over canvas layer","description":"## What
Composite text labels (node labels, edge labels, cluster titles) on top of canvas-rendered shapes. Canvas draws geometry in sub-cell dots; text must be rendered in the cell layer on top.

## Implementation
- Canvas rendering writes to a CanvasLayer (Braille/Block/HalfBlock dots).
- Text rendering writes to the Buffer cell layer (standard Cell writes).
- When compositing: cells with text take priority over canvas dots in the same cell. This is how canvas.rs already works — Painter writes to a separate pixel grid that gets merged into Buffer cells, and explicit cell writes override.
- Center node labels within the shape's usable interior area (accounting for shape geometry — diamond has triangular corners, circle has curved edges).
- Edge labels: position at midpoint of edge, write text centered.
- Wrap long labels using existing wrap_text function.

## Key Design Decision
- Do NOT try to render text as Braille dots. Cell-based text is always more readable.
- Background color under text should match the node fill for readability.

## Files
- crates/ftui-extras/src/mermaid_render.rs (add canvas_composite_labels)

## Tests

 {"id":"bd-ukp1f.5","title":"Canvas: triangular arrowhead rendering","description":"## What
Render arrowheads as filled triangles at edge endpoints using Painter polygon fill, instead of '>' / '<' / 'v' / '^' characters.

## Implementation
- Compute arrowhead direction from the last two waypoints of each edge.
- Triangle vertices: tip at endpoint, two base vertices perpendicular to edge direction, spread by configurable width (default 3-4 canvas pixels).
- Fill the triangle with the edge color.
- Support arrow types: normal (filled triangle), open (outline only), circle (small filled circle), cross (X mark), diamond (small filled diamond).

## Edge Cases
- Very short edges: skip arrowhead if edge length < arrowhead size.
- Bidirectional edges: arrowheads at both ends.
- Self-loops: arrowhead at the loop's return point.

## Files
- crates/ftui-extras/src/mermaid_render.rs (add canvas_render_arrowhead)
- crates/ftui-extras/src/canvas.rs (add polygon_fill if not present)

## Tests
